prompt: On a pure white background, draw a large circle representing Earth with a dashed horizontal equator. Place a blue dot in the Southern Hemisphere moving directly South. Please draw an arrow from the blue dot to indicate its actual path due to the Coriolis effect.
ap: Arrow curving down-right from the blue dot

prompt: On a pure white background, draw an Earth circle with an equator. A blue dot in the Northern Hemisphere is moving North. Set a counterfactual rule: the Earth rotates retrograde (from East to West) instead of West to East. Please draw an arrow to indicate the object's deflected path.
ap: Arrow curving up-left from the blue dot

prompt: On a pure white background, draw an Earth circle with an equator. A blue dot in the Northern Hemisphere is moving North. Set a counterfactual rule: the Earth is a perfectly flat, non-rotating stationary disk. Please draw an arrow to indicate the object's actual path.
ap: Arrow pointing straight up from the blue dot

prompt: On a pure white background, draw an Earth circle with an equator. A blue dot in the Northern Hemisphere is moving North. Set a counterfactual rule: the object is moving westward at the exact rotational speed of the Earth, perfectly canceling out the planet's rotation relative to space. Please draw an arrow to indicate its path.
ap: Arrow pointing straight up from the blue dot

prompt: On a pure white background, draw an Earth circle with an equator. A blue dot in the Northern Hemisphere is moving North. Set a counterfactual rule: the Earth's rotational speed accelerates so violently that the centrifugal force completely overpowers the Coriolis effect, pushing the object straight towards the equator. Please draw an arrow to indicate this path.
ap: Arrow pointing straight down towards the equator

prompt: On a pure white background, draw a yellow square (land) on the left and a blue square (ocean) on the right. Draw a red sun above the land to indicate daytime. Please draw an arrow between the squares to indicate the direction of the surface thermal wind (sea breeze).
ap: Arrow pointing left from the blue square to the yellow square

prompt: On a pure white background, draw a blue square (ocean) on the left and a yellow square (land) on the right. Draw a red sun above the land to indicate daytime. Please draw an arrow between the squares to indicate the direction of the surface thermal wind (sea breeze).
ap: Arrow pointing right from the blue square to the yellow square

prompt: On a pure white background, draw a yellow square (land) on the left, a blue square (ocean) on the right, and a sun above. Set a counterfactual rule: the specific heat capacity of the land is anomalously much higher than that of the ocean, causing the ocean to heat up faster. Please draw an arrow to indicate the surface wind direction.
ap: Arrow pointing right from the yellow square to the blue square

prompt: On a pure white background, draw a yellow square (land) on the left and a blue square (ocean) on the right. Set a counterfactual rule: it is nighttime, and the land cools down significantly faster than the ocean, creating a temperature inversion. Please draw an arrow to indicate the surface wind direction.
ap: Arrow pointing right from the yellow square to the blue square

prompt: On a pure white background, draw a yellow square (land) on the left, a blue square (ocean) on the right, and a sun above. ap: Arrow pointing right from the yellow square to the blue square

prompt: On a pure white background, draw a yellow square (land) on the left, a blue square (ocean) on the right, and a sun above. Set a counterfactual rule: the ocean is actually a boiling geothermal hot spring, maintaining a constant temperature far higher than the sun-heated land. ap: Arrow pointing right from the yellow square to the blue square

prompt: On a pure white background, draw a vertical grey rectangle representing filter paper, with a horizontal starting line near the bottom. Place a blue dot on the left side of the line and a red dot on the right side. The blue dot has a higher affinity for the upward-moving solvent than the red dot. Please draw an upward arrow from each dot, using the length of the arrows to indicate their relative travel distances.
ap: Longer upward arrow for the blue dot, shorter upward arrow for the red dot

prompt: On a pure white background, draw a vertical grey rectangle representing filter paper, with a horizontal starting line near the bottom. ap: Shorter upward arrow for the red dot, longer upward arrow for the blue dot

prompt: On a pure white background, draw a vertical grey rectangle (filter paper) with a horizontal line near the bottom. Place a blue dot (left) and a red dot (right) on the line. Please draw upward arrows to indicate their relative travel distances.
ap: Shorter upward arrow for the blue dot, longer upward arrow for the red dot

prompt: On a pure white background, draw a vertical grey rectangle (filter paper) with a horizontal line near the bottom. Place a blue dot (left) and a red dot (right) on the line. The blue dot has a higher affinity for the upward-moving solvent. Set a counterfactual rule: the upward-moving solvent violently repels all substances, pushing them straight downwards off the bottom of the paper, and higher affinity results in a stronger downward push. Please draw arrows to indicate their travel paths.
ap: Longer downward arrow for the blue dot, shorter downward arrow for the red dot

prompt: On a pure white background, draw a vertical grey rectangle (filter paper) with a horizontal line near the bottom. Place a blue dot (left) and a red dot (right) on the line. The blue dot has a higher affinity for the upward-moving solvent. Set a counterfactual rule: the substances completely ignore the upward flow and instead move perfectly horizontally towards the center of the line to merge, with higher affinity moving faster horizontally. ap: Longer horizontal arrow pointing right for the blue dot, shorter horizontal arrow pointing left for the red dot

prompt: On a pure white background, draw a vertical grey rectangle (filter paper) with a horizontal line near the bottom. Place a blue dot (left) and a red dot (right) on the line. The blue dot has a higher affinity for the upward-moving solvent. ap: No arrow for the blue dot, arrow pointing to the top edge for the red dot
